text: I submitted the same lab report twice with different file names and somehow got different marks both times.
Memorized the output of a program instead of understanding the logic. Passed. No regrets.
I asked a question in class just so the sir would be too busy answering to call on the people who didn't do the assignment. It worked perfectly.
I have a WhatsApp group called 'CSE Smart Friends' where I'm the only one asking questions.
Spent the whole night before the viva learning Stack Overflow answers by heart. Cleared it. Stack Overflow deserves my degree more than I do.
I've been in every group project but genuinely don't know what our final product does.
Cried in the bathroom on the first day of university because I thought everyone else was smarter than me. Three years later I found out most of them were crying too.
I told everyone I was 'revising' but I was watching The Office for the 6th time.
The friend I'd always text 'bhai notes bhejo' at 11 PM before an 8 AM exam — you saved my semester, probably multiple times. I never said it enough.
Submitted a project that was 70% GPT output with a few variable names changed. Got an A. I don't know how to feel about that.
My entire university social life happened in the 10 minutes between two classes outside that main block.
I pretended to understand pointers for two full years. I still don't. I passed both times. The system is broken.
There's one person in this batch who always came prepared and never complained. If you're reading this, you were the bar we all secretly tried to meet.
I once slept through an 8 AM class, panicked, and sprinted 15 minutes to uni only to find it was cancelled. I still consider that a win.
The energy in the hallway 5 minutes before results were announced was more intense than any hackathon I ever joined.
I didn't understand recursion until final year. I had passed three exams on it before that. I don't know what I wrote.
Four years and I still can't explain merge sort without drawing a tree on paper first.
I will miss the chai breaks more than any lecture. Nothing taught me more about friendship than standing at that dhaba doing absolutely nothing.
I used to think the people with clean notes and perfect attendance had it all figured out. Turns out they were just better at hiding the chaos.
There's a person in this batch I never properly spoke to. Four years, same batch, same classes. I wish I had.
